Renaissance Early Modern

Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
South Asian Traditions

Dharmakīrti

c. 600 – 660 CE

Reflexive awareness, nominalism, and Buddhist epistemology as a deep philosophy of consciousness.

His work must be read with care because he does not always speak from a single unqualified metaphysical standpoint. Much of his writing can be read in a position “common to Sautrāntika and Cittamātra”: external objects may be provisionally accepted, but what is immediately given in cognition is internal or mental. In the perception chapter of the Pramāṇavārttika, the idealist tendency becomes explicit, with Dharmakīrti arguing that what is cognizable is internal, that subject and object are not ultimately distinct, and that external objects are explanatorily redundant for cognition.

2. Historical position

Dharmakīrti’s dates remain debated. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that his biography is surrounded by hagiography and that scholars still debate whether to place him in the sixth or seventh century; Erich Frauwallner’s influential dating is 600–660 CE, though this is not conclusive. Tibetan sources connect him with South India and Nālandā, but the historical details are uncertain.

Philosophically, Dharmakīrti stands in the lineage of Dignāga, the founder of the Buddhist logico-epistemological tradition. Dignāga and Dharmakīrti became the central figures of what later scholarship calls the Buddhist “Epistemological School” or pramāṇavāda, a tradition that shaped pan-Indian debates on logic, language, universals, justification, perception, and the status of consciousness. Their school strongly influenced Mahāyāna Buddhism in Central Asia and especially Tibet.

Dharmakīrti’s major work is the Pramāṇavārttika, a philosophically ambitious commentary on Dignāga’s Pramāṇasamuccaya. The Pramāṇavārttika is the main source for his theory of knowledge, perception, inference, language, reality, and religious authority. His other works include the Pramāṇaviniścaya, Nyāyabindu, Hetubindu, Saṃbandhaparīkṣā, Vādanyāya, and Saṃtānāntarasiddhi, the last of which is especially important for the problem of other minds. These works came to be known in Tibet as the seven treatises on pramāṇa.

Within the history of philosophy of mind, Dharmakīrti occupies a position roughly analogous to a fusion of Aristotle, Hume, Kant, Brentano, Husserl, early analytic philosophy of language, and contemporary philosophy of consciousness, but with Buddhist premises. This comparison should not be taken to mean that he “anticipated” Western figures in a simplistic way. Rather, he developed a rigorous alternative trajectory in which philosophy of mind grows out of epistemology, meditative soteriology, nominalist metaphysics, and debates with Brahmanical realists, Mīmāṃsakas, Naiyāyikas, Cārvākas, and Mādhyamikas.

3. The metaphysical basis: causal particulars, not substances

Dharmakīrti’s philosophy of mind rests on a stark ontology. What is ultimately real is not a substance, a universal, a self, or an enduring object. What is ultimately real is a causally efficacious particular, a svalakṣaṇa. A real thing is something that has causal powers, arthakriyāsāmarthya. Universals, enduring substances, generic properties, and stable identities are conceptually constructed and conventionally useful, but they are not ultimately real.

This matters for mind because a mental event is not a state of an enduring soul. It is a momentary causal occurrence in a stream. The Buddhist picture rejects the idea that cognition belongs to a permanent subject. There is no underlying ātman that “has” experiences in the way a substance has properties. There are only psychophysical and cognitive continuities: sensations, perceptions, affective tones, intentions, memories, judgments, and reflexive awareness-events. The person is not denied at the conventional level, but personhood is reconstructed as a causal continuum rather than a metaphysical owner of experience.

Dharmakīrti therefore transforms the problem of mind. In much Western early modern philosophy, the central question becomes how an immaterial mind relates to a material body. In Dharmakīrti, the deeper question is how momentary cognitions can be valid, intentional, self-aware, causally continuous, and conceptually organized without an enduring self or real universals. Western mind-body dualism is the view that mind and body are fundamentally distinct kinds of things; Dharmakīrti is not a Cartesian substance dualist because he rejects an enduring mental substance, but he is also not a physicalist because he treats consciousness as explanatorily irreducible to insentient matter.

4. Pramāṇa: mind as normatively assessable cognition

The Sanskrit term pramāṇa is often translated as “valid cognition,” “reliable cognition,” or “means of knowledge.” Dharmakīrti defines a source of knowledge as a reliable cognition, avisaṃvādin jñāna. Reliability is not merely psychological confidence. A cognition is reliable when it arises through an appropriate causal or inferential route and enables successful engagement with what it presents. Later commentators such as Dharmottara sometimes express this in action-guiding terms: a valid cognition allows one to obtain the intended object. Tillemans argues that this should not be read as crude pragmatism about truth; it is better understood as a pragmatic theory of justification combined with a weak correspondence account, because successful action confirms that the object has the causal powers expected.

The Buddhist epistemologists accept only two basic pramāṇas: perception, pratyakṣa, and inference, anumāna. Perception is direct and nonconceptual; inference is conceptual and operates through signs, reasons, and generality. This division is grounded in a corresponding division of objects: perception apprehends particulars, while inference operates through universals or universal-like conceptual constructions.

This is already a philosophy of mind. It says that cognition has two fundamentally different modes. One mode is nonconceptual, causally constrained, immediate, and particular. The other is conceptual, language-involving, inferential, and generalizing. Human mental life is therefore structurally dual, not because there are two substances, but because there are two kinds of cognitive operation.

Ordinary experience, on this view, is already a composite. Seeing a blue patch is one thing; seeing “a blue cloth,” “my robe,” “a valuable object,” or “a thing I want” is another. The first is perceptual presentation. The second involves conceptual imputation, memory, linguistic habit, desire, and exclusionary classification.

5. Perception: nonconceptual awareness and the theory of mental aspects

Dharmakīrti’s theory of perception is among the most important parts of his philosophy of mind. Perception is not the ordinary act of recognizing objects under concepts. It is a nonconceptual awareness of the particular, prior to linguistic classification. Buddhist epistemologists sharply distinguish direct perception from conceptual elaboration, kalpanā. Memory and conceptual construction are not themselves direct sources of perceptual knowledge.

The key notion here is the ākāra, the “aspect,” “form,” or “appearance” of an object in cognition. A cognition does not simply stand outside an object and copy it from nowhere. It takes on an object-aspect. In the Sautrāntika register, an external object causally produces a perceptual image or aspect in consciousness. In the Yogācāra register, what is immediately available is only the cognitive aspect itself, and the alleged external object becomes unnecessary as an explanatory posit. The Stanford entry on Buddhist philosophy of mind summarizes Dignāga and Dharmakīrti as adopting an aspectual theory of cognition: cognition takes the form of what it cognizes, and the transition from raw perceptual experience to distinct recognition already involves conceptual elaboration.

This is why Dharmakīrti can be read as a representationalist, but not in the same way as many modern representationalists. The immediate object of awareness is not a public object already carved into commonsense categories; it is an internal or experiential aspect. Yet this aspect is not an inert “idea” floating in a private theater. It is causally embedded, action-guiding, and normatively assessable. It can lead to successful engagement or to error.

A useful way to formulate the view is this: perception supplies causally constrained phenomenal particulars; conceptual cognition supplies object-identity, classification, and linguistic articulation. The “world” we ordinarily inhabit is therefore not simply given. It is built through the interaction of nonconceptual presentation and conceptual construction.

6. Intentionality: object-directedness without a substantial subject

In modern philosophy, intentionality means the aboutness or object-directedness of mind: mental states are of, about, or directed toward things. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy defines intentionality as the power of minds and mental states to be about or represent things, properties, and states of affairs.

Dharmakīrti has a sophisticated theory of intentionality, though not in Brentano’s vocabulary. A cognition has an object-aspect, grāhyākāra, and a subject-aspect, grāhakākāra. The objective aspect captures what the cognition is of; the subjective aspect captures the fact that the cognition is present as a mode of awareness. The Buddhist epistemological tradition describes each cognitive event as having a dual aspect: a subjective appearance and an objective appearance. The subjective aspect is the self-intimating character of cognition; the objective aspect is its intentional content or object-directedness.

This gives Dharmakīrti a way to avoid two extremes. He avoids naïve realism, because what is immediately present is not simply a mind-independent object as such. But he also avoids a subject-object substance dualism, because there is no permanent subject standing over against an object. A cognition is internally structured as object-presenting and self-presenting. Subjectivity is a feature of the event, not the activity of an enduring ego.

This is one of Dharmakīrti’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind: he separates first-personal givenness from the metaphysical self. There can be subjectivity without a subject-substance. There can be self-awareness without a self.

7. Reflexive awareness: svasaṃvedana

The doctrine of svasaṃvedana or svasaṃvitti is central to extracting a philosophy of consciousness from Dharmakīrti. It is often translated as “self-awareness,” “reflexive awareness,” or “self-cognition,” but the translation “self-awareness” can mislead. It does not mean awareness of an enduring self. It means that a conscious event is aware of itself in the very act of being aware of its object.

The Dignāga-Dharmakīrti tradition accepts reflexive awareness in the Yogācāra sense of self-luminosity, often illustrated by the image of a lamp that illuminates both other things and itself. The point is not that consciousness is an object among objects. The point is that consciousness does not occur “in the dark.” A conscious episode is not merely a causal registration; it is manifest to itself.

Dharmakīrti’s position can be stated in four theses.

First, every conscious cognition is self-intimating. It is not merely directed outward; it is also immediately present as occurring.

Second, this reflexivity is same-order, not higher-order. A separate second cognition is not required to make the first cognition conscious. If every cognition needed another cognition to reveal it, one would face a regress: the second cognition would need a third, the third a fourth, and so on. Contemporary scholarship explicitly compares Dignāga’s and Dharmakīrti’s regress arguments with higher-order theories of consciousness.

Third, reflexive awareness is preconceptual. It is not the judgment “I am aware of seeing blue.” That judgment is conceptual and self-attributive. Reflexive awareness is the more primitive fact that the seeing is manifest as an experience.

Fourth, reflexive awareness grounds the possibility of epistemic assessment. Dharmakīrti’s Pramāṇaviniścaya says, in effect, that if ascertaining were not itself directly perceived, object-perception would not be established. In other words, cognition’s ability to disclose an object depends on its own being disclosed.

This doctrine places Dharmakīrti close to what contemporary philosophers call same-order or self-representational theories of consciousness, and close to phenomenological accounts of prereflective self-consciousness. It contrasts sharply with higher-order theories, which explain consciousness by appeal to a higher-order representation directed at a first-order mental state.

8. The no-self doctrine and the construction of “I”

Dharmakīrti inherits the Buddhist denial of a permanent self, anātman. Buddhist theories of mind generally analyze persons into physical and psychological constituents and processes rather than positing an immutable self.

But Dharmakīrti’s reflexive-awareness theory prevents this no-self doctrine from becoming a crude eliminativism about subjectivity. The self is not real, but experience is still first-personally manifest. What is rejected is not consciousness, not phenomenal character, not memory, not practical agency, and not the first-personal structure of experience. What is rejected is the inference from these features to an enduring owner.

The “I” is a conceptual construction imposed on a causal continuum. A mental stream contains memory, affect, intention, bodily orientation, and reflexive awareness. Conceptual thought then appropriates this stream as “me,” “mine,” or “I.” This resembles, but is not identical to, Hume’s later bundle theory. Hume claims that we never directly apprehend a substantial self and that the self is a bundle of perceptions; Dharmakīrti’s view is similar in its anti-substantialism, but differs by giving reflexive awareness a foundational role and by embedding the analysis in a Buddhist causal and soteriological framework.

The result is a powerful model: first-personal consciousness does not require an ego-substance. The unity of experience is not the unity of a self; it is the causal, mnemonic, affective, and conceptual organization of a stream.

9. Concepts, language, and apoha

Dharmakīrti’s theory of mind cannot be separated from his theory of concepts. Since only particulars are ultimately real, universals cannot be real entities. Yet thought and language require generality. We think “cow,” “blue,” “person,” “pain,” “mind,” and “self.” How is this possible if there are no real universals?

The answer is apoha, usually translated as “exclusion.” A concept does not grasp a real universal shared by many particulars. Instead, it functions by excluding what is other. “Cow” does not refer to a real universal cowhood; it operates by excluding non-cows. “Blue” does not pick out a real universal blueness; it groups particulars through exclusion of the non-blue.

This is not mere arbitrary nominalism. Dharmakīrti gives a causal story of concept formation. Particular blue things produce perceptual images; these images, together with habits and dispositions, lead to the same judgment, “This is blue.” A word becomes tied to a class of particulars because those particulars play the relevant causal role in producing the cognition and linguistic use. The Stanford entry explicitly compares this to causal theories of reference associated with Kripke and Putnam, while emphasizing Dharmakīrti’s distinctive Buddhist framework.

The philosophy-of-mind significance is large. Dharmakīrti gives an account of how the mind constructs stable objects and kinds from unstable particulars. He thereby offers a theory of categorization, semantic content, concept acquisition, and cognitive error.

Conceptuality is necessary for practical life, inference, communication, memory, and debate. But it is also a source of distortion because it presents constructed generalities as if they were ultimately real. The mind is therefore both adaptive and delusive. It tracks causal patterns well enough for successful action, yet it reifies those patterns into substances, universals, selves, and enduring objects.

10. Error, illusion, and ordinary realism

Dharmakīrti’s distinction between perception and conception yields a subtle theory of error. Raw perceptual presentation is not yet the full-blown false judgment. Error enters when conceptual cognition misclassifies, superimposes, or reifies. A mirage, dream, hallucination, or illusion can be analyzed as a real mental event with an appearing aspect, but a mistaken interpretation of what that aspect indicates.

This is why Dharmakīrti can treat ordinary realism as pragmatically functional but philosophically unstable. At the everyday level, we speak and act as if there are stable objects, selves, and kinds. At the ultimate level, these are conceptual constructions over momentary causal events. The goal is not to deny conventional life, but to understand its cognitive architecture.

This gives his philosophy of mind a diagnostic function. The mind is not merely a neutral mirror. It is an active constructor of the world of ordinary experience. Its deepest errors are not isolated false beliefs; they are structural habits of reification.

11. The status of the external world

Dharmakīrti’s relation to external-world realism is one of the most debated parts of his philosophy. On one reading, he is a Sautrāntika representational realist: external objects exist, but they are known only through internal cognitive representations. On another reading, he is ultimately a Yogācāra idealist: external objects are not needed and cannot be established; what is given are mental appearances. The Stanford entry presents the issue as a shift or alternation: most of Dharmakīrti’s work can be read in a way acceptable to both external realists and idealists, but the later part of the perception chapter argues that what is immediately cognizable is internal and that external objects become explanatorily redundant.

The philosophy-of-mind issue is this: what is the immediate object of awareness? Dharmakīrti’s answer is not the ordinary external object as common sense imagines it. The immediate object is an appearing cognitive aspect. If external objects are accepted, they are causal conditions, not directly presented objects. If idealism is accepted, even those external causes are unnecessary.

Compared with Western philosophy, this resembles some aspects of early modern representationalism, sense-datum theory, phenomenalism, and transcendental idealism, but it differs in its Buddhist commitments to momentariness, no-self, and conceptual exclusion. The Buddhist epistemologists’ representational model has even been compared to Western representationalism in Descartes and Locke.

12. Other minds

Dharmakīrti wrote a work called Saṃtānāntarasiddhi, “Proof of Other Minds” or “Establishment of Other Mental Continua.” The work is central because idealist or representation-centered theories always risk solipsism: if I am immediately aware only of my own cognitive appearances, how can I know that there are other streams of consciousness? EAST lists Sanskrit fragments, reconstructions, and translations of this work, including Stcherbatsky’s English rendering under the title “Establishment of the Existence of Other Minds.”

The basic Dharmakīrtian strategy is inferential. Other minds are not perceived directly in the way one’s own current awareness is reflexively manifest. They are inferred from patterns of bodily and verbal behavior, by analogy with the observed relation between one’s own mental activity and one’s own actions. Scholarship on the Buddhist problem of other minds describes the Vijñānavādin strategy as explaining our awareness of other subjects through inference, and specifically discusses Dharmakīrti’s Saṃtānāntarasiddhi in this context.

This is close to the Western “argument from analogy” or “inference to the best explanation” for other minds. The traditional Western problem asks how one can know that others have minds if one observes only their behavior; standard responses include analogy, best explanation, criteria, and direct perception accounts.

Dharmakīrti’s solution is both powerful and vulnerable. It is powerful because it gives a non-solipsistic account consistent with the privacy of reflexive awareness. It is vulnerable because, if the external body is itself only an appearance within cognition, the inference to another independent mindstream becomes difficult to secure. Later Buddhist idealists and their critics press exactly this problem.

13. Mind and body: against physicalism, without Cartesian dualism

Dharmakīrti rejects the reduction of consciousness to insentient physical elements. In arguments connected with rebirth and the continuity of consciousness, he argues against the view that consciousness can arise from purely material elements. The Stanford entry on Buddhist philosophy of mind summarizes his objection: if consciousness arose from the four elements or some special transformation of them, then consciousness should arise wherever such elements occur; and even if one restricts its arising to a particular time, one still has not explained how sentience arises from the insentient.

From a contemporary standpoint, this argument will not convince a physicalist without further premises. Modern identity theory, for example, holds that mental states and processes are identical with brain states and processes. Dharmakīrti does not have modern neuroscience, and his causal principles differ from those of contemporary philosophy of mind. Still, his position is philosophically important because it is neither Cartesian substance dualism nor materialist reductionism.

He denies a permanent mental substance. He denies a soul. But he also denies that consciousness can be explained as a product of non-conscious matter. The result is a process-based anti-physicalism: consciousness is a stream of sentient events conditioned by previous sentient events, not an enduring immaterial thing and not a mere physical byproduct.

This gives Dharmakīrti a distinctive place in global philosophy of mind. He is not a dualist in the standard Western sense, not an idealist in Berkeley’s theological sense, not a materialist, and not a panpsychist. His closest category is perhaps event-based Buddhist phenomenal idealism or causal mental-stream theory.

14. Comparison with Western developments

Descartes and early modern representationalism

Descartes makes the mind-body problem central: how can thinking substance and extended substance relate? Western dualism treats mind and body as fundamentally distinct kinds, and the modern mind-body problem becomes especially acute after seventeenth-century mechanistic science.

Dharmakīrti differs sharply. He does not posit a thinking substance. He has no Cartesian ego. He also does not begin from the certainty of “I think.” He begins from valid cognition, causal efficacy, and the structure of awareness. Still, there is convergence: both Dharmakīrti and early modern representationalists are concerned with the mediated relation between experience and world. The difference is that Dharmakīrti uses this mediation to undermine enduring selfhood and external-object realism, while Descartes uses self-certainty to ground a metaphysics of mind.

Locke and representational ideas

Locke’s empiricism treats ideas as immediate objects of the mind. Dharmakīrti similarly treats cognitive aspects as immediately present, but he does not build a theory of ideas as contents of a persisting subject. For Dharmakīrti, the “idea” or aspect is a momentary event in a stream; it is not owned by a substantial self.

Hume and bundle theory

Hume is the closest Western early modern comparison on selfhood. Hume argues that introspection never reveals a substantial self, only particular perceptions, leading to a bundle theory of the self. Dharmakīrti’s Buddhist no-self view is structurally similar, but more systematic in two respects: it is tied to a general ontology of momentary causal particulars, and it includes a robust theory of reflexive awareness. Hume gives us perceptions without a self; Dharmakīrti gives us reflexively manifest cognitions without a self.

Kant and apperception

Kant rejects both simple empiricist bundle theory and dogmatic metaphysical self-knowledge. Dharmakīrti’s relation to Kant is complex. Like Kant, he understands ordinary objectivity as involving mental contribution. Unlike Kant, he does not posit a transcendental unity of apperception as a necessary formal condition of experience. The Buddhist tradition has nevertheless been compared with Kantian debates about apperception, especially because svasaṃvedana raises the question of whether cognition must be self-related in order to be cognition at all.

Brentano, Husserl, Sartre, and phenomenology

Brentano reintroduced intentionality into modern philosophy as a mark of the mental; Husserl developed intentionality into phenomenology; Sartre defended a form of prereflective self-consciousness. Dharmakīrti’s theory converges strongly with this trajectory. The SEP explicitly notes that Indian Buddhist reflexive awareness has been compared with Aristotle, Brentano, Sartre, Kant, Husserl, and Merleau-Ponty.

The strongest convergence is with prereflective self-consciousness. Phenomenologists argue that experience is not first unconscious and then made conscious by reflection. Rather, experience is already implicitly self-given. This is very close to Dharmakīrti’s claim that cognition is self-intimating. The difference is that phenomenology often remains methodologically descriptive, whereas Dharmakīrti embeds the claim in a Buddhist epistemological, metaphysical, and soteriological system.

Higher-order theories of consciousness

Contemporary higher-order theories explain a mental state’s being conscious by appeal to a higher-order representation of that state. Dharmakīrti is better read as a same-order theorist. Consciousness is not made conscious by another state; it is reflexively manifest in its own occurrence. His regress arguments can be interpreted as an early and powerful objection to higher-order models: if awareness always requires another awareness, consciousness is never actually established.

Contemporary physicalism

Modern physicalism claims, roughly, that everything is physical. The identity theory holds that mental processes are identical to brain processes. Dharmakīrti rejects such reduction in principle, but not by positing a soul. His objection is that sentience cannot be explained by insentient causes alone. Contemporary philosophers may reject his causal premise, but his view remains philosophically significant because it articulates a non-Cartesian anti-reductionism.

Philosophy of language and semantic externalism

Dharmakīrti’s apoha theory has striking affinities with later debates about reference, classification, and meaning. His causal account of how particulars produce perceptual images, judgments, and word use resembles causal theories of reference, though his denial of real universals and his exclusion theory make his semantics more nominalist and cognitive than Kripke’s or Putnam’s.

15. The reconstructed Dharmakīrtian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Dharmakīrti can be organized into twelve theses. 1. Mind is not a substance. It is a stream of momentary cognitive events. 2. Mental events are real insofar as they are causally efficacious. Their reality is not grounded in being owned by a self. 3. Consciousness is intentional. A cognition presents an object-aspect. 4. Consciousness is reflexive. A cognition is aware of itself in the same event by which it presents its object. 5. Self-awareness is not ego-awareness. The reflexivity of cognition does not establish an ātman. 6. Perception is nonconceptual. It discloses particulars or immediate cognitive appearances, not universals or linguistically structured objects. 7. Conceptual cognition is constructive. It organizes experience through exclusions, habits, memory, and linguistic practices. 8. Universals are not ultimately real. They are useful conceptual fictions. 9. Error arises through conceptual superimposition and reification. The mind mistakes its constructed categories for ultimately real structures. 10. The external world is at least epistemically mediated and, on the stronger Yogācāra reading, metaphysically unnecessary. 11. Other minds are known inferentially, not by the same reflexive immediacy through which one’s present cognition is known. 12. Liberation requires cognitive transformation: one must understand how conceptual construction, self-imputation, and reification generate suffering.

This system is a kind of phenomenological nominalism joined to causal epistemology. It is phenomenological because it begins from the structure of awareness. It is nominalist because it denies real universals. It is causal because validity, reference, concept formation, and reality are all explained through causal efficacy. It is Buddhist because the entire system is ordered toward dismantling ignorance, especially the reification of self and world.

16. Major interpretive tensions

The first tension is pragmatism versus correspondence. Dharmakīrti often links valid cognition with successful action, which has led some interpreters to call him a pragmatist. A more careful reading treats him as offering a pragmatic account of justification rather than a pragmatic theory of truth. Successful action matters because it confirms causal efficacy, not because truth simply means usefulness.

The second tension is realism versus idealism. Dharmakīrti often writes in a way compatible with external realism, but his arguments in the perception chapter push toward Yogācāra idealism. Whether this is a strategic alternation, a developmental shift, or a layered system remains debated.

The third tension is reflexive awareness versus emptiness. Mādhyamika critics such as Candrakīrti object that if reflexive awareness is taken as intrinsically self-characterizing, it violates the universal emptiness of phenomena. The SEP notes that Candrakīrti’s critique targets the idea that there is a class of cognitive events that reveal themselves intrinsically without dependence on anything else.

The fourth tension is nonconceptual perception. Dharmakīrti needs nonconceptual perception to ground knowledge, but he also recognizes that ordinary object-experience is conceptually structured. This raises a version of what modern philosophers might call the problem of the given: can a nonconceptual state justify conceptual belief? Dharmakīrti’s answer is causal and practical: perception grounds later judgments because it is causally connected to particulars and because successful action confirms the relevant causal powers.

The fifth tension is other minds. If all immediate objects are internal cognitive aspects, other minds are not directly perceived. Dharmakīrti’s inferential solution is elegant, but under strong idealism it becomes fragile.

17. Why Dharmakīrti matters for philosophy of mind today

Dharmakīrti matters because he provides a sophisticated alternative to the dominant Western framing of the mind. The Western tradition often makes the mind-body problem central; Dharmakīrti makes the cognition-validity-conceptualization problem central. The result is a philosophy of mind in which consciousness, intentionality, self-awareness, semantics, and ontology are inseparable.

His most important contribution is the possibility of subjectivity without a subject-substance. He shows how one might preserve the first-personal character of experience while denying the metaphysical self. This is highly relevant to contemporary debates over minimal selfhood, prereflective self-consciousness, no-self theories, and the relation between phenomenal consciousness and self-representation.

His second major contribution is the integration of perception and concept formation. Dharmakīrti does not treat concepts as passive labels applied to a ready-made world. Concepts actively construct the ordinary world by grouping particulars through exclusion. This gives him a powerful theory of cognitive construction that speaks to current philosophy of perception, cognitive science, philosophy of language, and social ontology.

His third contribution is his same-order reflexive theory of consciousness. Against higher-order theories, he argues that consciousness does not need a second mental state to become conscious. Against naïve realism, he argues that what is immediately present is an experiential aspect. Against ego theories, he argues that reflexivity does not imply a self.

His fourth contribution is methodological. Dharmakīrti shows that philosophy of mind need not begin from introspective certainty, linguistic analysis, neuroscience, or metaphysical dualism. It can begin from the norms of reliable cognition and ask: what must mind be like for knowledge, error, inference, language, memory, and liberation to be possible?

18. Best way to study Dharmakīrti for philosophy of mind

The most important primary materials are the Pramāṇavārttika, especially the chapters on perception, inference, and the establishment of valid cognition; the Pramāṇaviniścaya for refined epistemological formulations; the Nyāyabindu and Hetubindu for compact logical and epistemological doctrine; the Pramāṇavārttikasvavṛtti for apoha and inference; and the Saṃtānāntarasiddhi for other minds. EAST’s bibliographic entries list editions and translations of several of these works, including the Pramāṇavārttikavṛtti/Svavṛtti and Saṃtānāntarasiddhi.

For secondary literature, the most important modern interpreters include Georges Dreyfus, John Dunne, Tom Tillemans, Birgit Kellner, Dan Arnold, Christian Coseru, Mark Siderits, Jonardon Ganeri, Eli Franco, Vincent Eltschinger, Ernst Steinkellner, and Sara McClintock. The relevant debates concern Dharmakīrti’s idealism, apoha, self-awareness, causal theory, anti-physicalism, and Tibetan reception.

The most fruitful research program would read Dharmakīrti alongside four Western clusters: Hume on no-self and bundle theory; Kant and post-Kantian debates on apperception; Brentano, Husserl, and Sartre on intentionality and prereflective self-awareness; and contemporary analytic debates over higher-order consciousness, representationalism, nonconceptual content, and physicalism. The comparison should not assimilate Dharmakīrti into Western categories too quickly. His deepest originality lies precisely in how he rearranges the field: mind is not first a substance, not first a brain process, and not first a linguistic subject. It is a reflexively manifest, causally embedded, momentary cognitive event within a stream, whose conceptual habits construct the world it then mistakes for ultimate reality.

PreviousDignāga NextŚaṅkara

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
